1.text: King Benjamin had no more conflicts throughout all the land of Zarahemla among his people. He had continual peace for the rest of his days.

He had three sons: Mosiah, Helorum, and Helaman. He had them taught in all the language of his fathers, so they could become men of understanding. He wanted them to know about the prophecies spoken by their ancestors, which were delivered to them by the hand of the Lord.

He also taught them about the records engraved on the brass plates, saying: My sons, I want you to remember that if it were not for these plates, which contain these records and commandments, we would have suffered in ignorance even now, not knowing the mysteries of God.

It would not have been possible for our father Lehi to remember all these things and teach them to his children without the help of these plates. Having been taught in the language of the Egyptians, he could read these engravings and teach them to his children. That way they could teach them to their children, fulfilling the commandments of God down to the present time.

I tell you, my sons, if it were not for these things, which have been kept and preserved by the hand of God so that we could read and understand his mysteries and always have his commandments before our eyes, even our fathers would have fallen into unbelief, and we would have been like our brothers the Lamanites, who know nothing about these things. They do not even believe them when they are taught, because of the traditions of their fathers, which are not correct.

My sons, I want you to remember that what I say is true. Also, these records are true. And the plates of Nephi, which contain the records and words of our fathers from the time they left Jerusalem until now — these are also true. We can know they are certain because we have them before our eyes.

Now, my sons, I want you to remember to search them diligently so you may benefit from it. I want you to keep the commandments of God so you may prosper in the land, according to the promises the Lord made to our fathers.
King Benjamin taught his sons many more things that are not written in this book.

When King Benjamin finished teaching his sons, he grew old. He realized he would soon have to go the way of all the earth. Therefore, he thought it was important to give the kingdom to one of his sons.

So he had Mosiah brought before him, and these are the words he spoke to him: My son, I want you to make a proclamation throughout all this land, among all this people — the people of Zarahemla and the people of Mosiah who live in the land — so they may be gathered together. Tomorrow I will proclaim to this my people, with my own mouth, that you are a king and ruler over this people whom the Lord our God has given us.

And furthermore, I will give this people a name that will distinguish them above all the people the Lord God has brought out of the land of Jerusalem. I do this because they have been a diligent people in keeping the commandments of the Lord.
I give them a name that will never be blotted out, except through transgression.

And furthermore I say to you, if this highly favored people of the Lord should fall into transgression and become a wicked and adulterous people, the Lord will deliver them up so they become weak like their brothers. He will no longer preserve them by his matchless and marvelous power as he has preserved our fathers until now.
For I tell you that if he had not stretched out his arm to preserve our fathers, they would have fallen into the hands of the Lamanites and become victims of their hatred.
King Benjamin finished speaking these words to his son and gave him charge concerning all the affairs of the kingdom.

He also gave him charge concerning the records engraved on the brass plates, the plates of Nephi, the sword of Laban, and the ball or director that led their fathers through the wilderness. It was prepared by the hand of the Lord to guide them, each one according to the attention and diligence they gave to him.

When they were unfaithful, they did not prosper or progress on their journey. Instead they were driven back and brought the displeasure of God upon them. Therefore they were struck with famine and severe afflictions to stir them up to remember their duty.

Now Mosiah went and did as his father had commanded him. He proclaimed to all the people throughout the land of Zarahemla that they should gather themselves together, to go up to the temple to hear the words his father would speak to them.

2.text: After Mosiah had done as his father commanded and made the proclamation throughout the land, the people gathered together throughout the land to go up to the temple to hear King Benjamin's words.
There were a great number of them — so many that they did not count them — because they had multiplied greatly and become numerous in the land.
They also brought the firstborn of their flocks to offer sacrifice and burnt offerings according to the law of Moses.

They also came to give thanks to the Lord their God, who had brought them out of the land of Jerusalem, who had delivered them from their enemies, who had appointed just men to be their teachers and also a just man to be their king. He had established peace in the land of Zarahemla and had taught them to keep the commandments of God, so they could rejoice and be filled with love toward God and all people.

When they arrived at the temple, they pitched their tents around it, each family separate from one another — his wife, his sons and daughters, and their sons and daughters, from the oldest to the youngest.
They pitched their tents around the temple, each man with his tent door facing the temple, so they could stay in their tents and hear King Benjamin's words.
Because the multitude was so large, King Benjamin could not teach them all inside the temple walls. So he had a tower built so his people could hear the words he would speak to them.

He began to speak to his people from the tower. Not all of them could hear his words because of the large crowd. Therefore he had his words written down and sent among those who were not close enough to hear his voice, so they could also receive his words.

These are the words he spoke and had written: My brothers and sisters, all of you who have gathered together — you who can hear my words that I will speak to you today — I have not commanded you to come here to take my words lightly. Rather, I want you to open your ears and hear, open your hearts so you may understand, and open your minds so the mysteries of God may be revealed to you.
I have not commanded you to come here so you would fear me, or think that I myself am more than a mortal man.

I am like yourselves — subject to all kinds of weaknesses of body and mind. Yet I have been chosen by this people and consecrated by my father, and the Lord's hand allowed me to be a ruler and king over this people. I have been kept and preserved by his matchless power to serve you with all the might, mind, and strength the Lord has granted me.
I tell you, I have been allowed to spend my days in your service, even up to this time, and I have not sought gold or silver or any kind of wealth from you.

I have not allowed you to be confined in dungeons, or that you should make slaves of one another, or that you should murder, plunder, steal, or commit adultery. I have not allowed you to commit any kind of wickedness. I have taught you to keep the commandments of the Lord in all things he has commanded you.

And I have even worked with my own hands to serve you, so you would not be burdened with taxes, and so nothing would come upon you that would be hard to bear. You are all witnesses of everything I say this day.
Yet, my people, I have not done these things to boast or to accuse you. I say these things so you may know that I can answer with a clear conscience before God this day.
I tell you, when I say I have spent my days in your service, I am not boasting, because I have only been serving God.
I tell you these things so you may learn wisdom — so you may learn that when you are in the service of your fellow beings, you are only in the service of your God.
You have called me your king. If I, whom you call your king, have labored to serve you, then shouldn't you labor to serve one another?
And if I, whom you call your king, who has spent his days serving you and yet has only been serving God, deserve any thanks from you — then how much more should you thank your heavenly King!

I say to you, my people, if you should give all the thanks and praise your whole soul is capable of to that God who has created you, kept you, and preserved you, and caused you to rejoice, and granted that you should live in peace with one another —

I say to you, if you served him who created you from the beginning and is preserving you from day to day by giving you breath so you can live, move, and act according to your own will — even supporting you from one moment to the next — I say, if you served him with all your whole souls, you would still be unprofitable servants.

And all he requires of you is to keep his commandments. He has promised you that if you keep his commandments, you will prosper in the land. He never changes from what he has said. Therefore, if you keep his commandments, he blesses you and makes you prosper.
In the first place, he created you and granted you your lives, for which you owe him.

Secondly, he requires that you do as he has commanded you. If you do, he immediately blesses you. So he has already paid you. You are still indebted to him, and are now and will be forever and ever. So what do you have to boast about?

And now I ask, can you say anything for yourselves? I answer you: No. You cannot say you are even as much as the dust of the earth. Yet you were created from the dust of the earth, and it belongs to him who created you.
And I, whom you call your king, am no better than you are, for I am also from the dust. You can see that I am old and am about to give up this mortal body to its mother earth.

Therefore, as I told you that I had served you, walking with a clear conscience before God, I have now caused you to gather together so I might be found blameless, and so your blood would not come upon me when I stand to be judged by God of the things he has commanded me concerning you.

I tell you that I have caused you to gather together so I might cleanse my garments of your blood, at this time when I am about to go down to my grave. I want to go down in peace, and may my immortal spirit join the choirs above in singing the praises of a just God.
Furthermore, I tell you that I have caused you to gather together so I could declare to you that I can no longer be your teacher or your king.

For even now, my whole body trembles greatly while trying to speak to you. But the Lord God supports me and has allowed me to speak to you. He has commanded me to declare to you today that my son Mosiah is a king and ruler over you.

Now, my people, I want you to continue doing as you have done. As you have kept my commandments and the commandments of my father and have prospered and been kept from falling into the hands of your enemies — if you keep the commandments of my son, or the commandments of God that will be given to you through him, you will prosper in the land, and your enemies will have no power over you.
But, O my people, beware that no conflicts arise among you and that you do not choose to obey the evil spirit, which was spoken of by my father Mosiah.

For woe is pronounced upon the one who chooses to obey that spirit. If he chooses to obey it, and remains in his sins and dies, he drinks damnation to his own soul. He receives as his wages an everlasting punishment, having broken the law of God against his own knowledge.

I say to you, there is no one among you — except your little children who have not been taught about these things — who does not know that you are eternally indebted to your heavenly Father, to give him everything you have and are. You have also been taught about the records containing the prophecies spoken by the holy prophets, down to the time our father Lehi left Jerusalem.
And also everything that has been spoken by our fathers until now. And what they spoke was commanded them by the Lord. Therefore, their words are just and true.

Now I say to you, my people, if after you have known and been taught all these things, you should disobey and go against what has been spoken — if you withdraw yourselves from the Spirit of the Lord, so that it has no place in you to guide you in wisdom's paths where you may be blessed, prospered, and preserved —

I say to you, the person who does this comes out in open rebellion against God. He chooses to obey the evil spirit and becomes an enemy to all righteousness. Therefore, the Lord has no place in him, for he does not dwell in unholy temples.

If that person does not repent and remains and dies an enemy to God, the demands of divine justice awaken his immortal soul to a vivid sense of his own guilt, causing him to shrink from the presence of the Lord. His heart is filled with guilt, pain, and anguish, which is like an unquenchable fire whose flame rises up forever and ever.
And now I say to you, mercy has no claim on that person. Therefore his final destiny is to endure never-ending torment.

O all you old men, and also you young men, and you little children who can understand my words — for I have spoken plainly to you so you can understand — I pray that you will awake to a remembrance of the terrible situation of those who have fallen into transgression.

Furthermore, I want you to consider the blessed and happy state of those who keep the commandments of God. For they are blessed in all things, both temporal and spiritual. If they hold out faithfully to the end, they are received into heaven, where they may dwell with God in a state of never-ending happiness. O remember, remember that these things are true, for the Lord God has spoken it.

3.text: And again, my people, I want to call your attention, for I have something more to say. I have things to tell you about what is to come.
The things I will tell you were made known to me by an angel from God. He said to me: Wake up! And I woke up, and he stood before me.
He said to me: Wake up, and hear the words I will tell you. I have come to declare to you the glad tidings of great joy.

The Lord has heard your prayers, has judged your righteousness, and has sent me to declare to you that you may rejoice and that you may declare these things to your people so they also may be filled with joy.

For the time is coming, and is not far away, when the Lord Omnipotent who reigns — who was and is from all eternity to all eternity — will come down from heaven among the children of men. He will dwell in a body of clay and go among the people, performing mighty miracles — healing the sick, raising the dead, making the lame walk, the blind see, and the deaf hear, and curing all kinds of diseases.
He will cast out devils, or the evil spirits that live in the hearts of the children of men.

He will endure temptations, body pain, hunger, thirst, and exhaustion — even more than man can suffer without dying. For blood will come from every pore, so great will be his anguish for the wickedness and abominations of his people.
He will be called Jesus Christ, the Son of God, the Father of heaven and earth, the Creator of all things from the beginning. His mother will be called Mary.

He comes to his own people so salvation might come to the children of men through faith in his name. Even after all this, they will consider him a man and say he has a devil, and will whip him and crucify him.
He will rise from the dead on the third day and will stand to judge the world. All this is done so that a righteous judgment might come upon the children of men.
For his blood atones for the sins of those who have fallen by the transgression of Adam, who have died not knowing the will of God concerning them, or who have ignorantly sinned.
But woe to the one who knows he rebels against God! Salvation comes to no such person unless he repents and has faith in the Lord Jesus Christ.

The Lord God has sent his holy prophets among all the children of men to declare these things to every family, nation, and language, so that whoever believes that Christ will come might receive forgiveness of their sins and rejoice with exceedingly great joy, as if he had already come among them.
Yet the Lord God saw that his people were a stubborn people, so he appointed a law for them — the law of Moses.

He showed them many signs, wonders, types, and shadows concerning his coming. Holy prophets also spoke to them about his coming. Yet they hardened their hearts and did not understand that the law of Moses has no value without the atonement of his blood.

Even if it were possible for little children to sin, they could not be saved. But I say to you, they are blessed. For just as the fall came upon all mankind through Adam — or by nature — the blood of Christ also atones for their sins.
Furthermore, there will be no other name given, no other way or means by which salvation can come to the children of men, except in and through the name of Christ, the Lord Omnipotent.

For he judges, and his judgment is just. The infant who dies in infancy does not perish. But men drink damnation to their own souls unless they humble themselves and become as little children and believe that salvation was, is, and is to come in and through the atoning blood of Christ, the Lord Omnipotent.

For the natural man is an enemy to God, and has been from the fall of Adam, and will be forever and ever, unless he yields to the promptings of the Holy Spirit and puts off the natural man and becomes a saint through the atonement of Christ the Lord. He must become as a child — submissive, meek, humble, patient, full of love, willing to submit to all things that the Lord sees fit to inflict upon him, just as a child submits to his father.
Furthermore I say to you, the time will come when the knowledge of a Savior will spread throughout every nation, family, language, and people.
And when that time comes, no one will be found blameless before God except little children, and only through repentance and faith in the name of the Lord God Omnipotent.

And even at this time, when you have taught your people the things the Lord your God has commanded you, even then they are found no more blameless in the sight of God, except according to the words I have spoken to you.
And now I have spoken the words the Lord God has commanded me.

The Lord has said: These words will stand as a bright testimony against this people at the judgment day. They will be judged by them, every person according to his works, whether they are good or evil.

If they are evil, they are consigned to an awful view of their own guilt and abominations, which causes them to shrink from the presence of the Lord into a state of misery and endless torment, from which they can never return. Therefore they have drunk damnation to their own souls.

They have drunk from the cup of God's wrath, which justice could no more deny them than it could deny that Adam should fall for eating the forbidden fruit. Therefore, mercy could never again claim them.
Their torment is like a lake of fire and brimstone, whose flames are unquenchable and whose smoke rises up forever and ever. This is what the Lord has commanded me. Amen.

4.text: When King Benjamin finished speaking the words delivered to him by the angel of the Lord, he looked around at the multitude. They had fallen to the earth, for the fear of the Lord had come upon them.

They had seen themselves in their own sinful state — even less than the dust of the earth. They all cried out with one voice, saying: Have mercy on us! Apply the atoning blood of Christ so we may receive forgiveness of our sins and our hearts may be purified. We believe in Jesus Christ, the Son of God, who created heaven and earth and all things, who will come down among the children of men.

After they had spoken these words, the Spirit of the Lord came upon them and they were filled with joy. They received a remission of their sins and had peace of conscience because of their exceedingly great faith in Jesus Christ who would come, according to the words King Benjamin had spoken to them.

King Benjamin opened his mouth again and began to speak to them, saying: My friends, my brothers and sisters, my family and my people, I would again call your attention so you may hear and understand the rest of my words.
For if the knowledge of God's goodness has at this time awakened you to a sense of your nothingness and your worthless and fallen state —

I say to you, if you have come to know the goodness of God, his matchless power, his wisdom, his patience, and his long-suffering toward the children of men — and also the atonement prepared from the foundation of the world, so that salvation might come to the one who puts his trust in the Lord and is diligent in keeping his commandments and continues in faith to the end of his life, meaning the life of the mortal body —

I say that this is the person who receives salvation through the atonement prepared from the foundation of the world for all mankind who have ever lived since the fall of Adam, or who now live, or who will ever live, even to the end of the world.
This is the way salvation comes. There is no other salvation besides what I have told you about, and there are no other conditions by which people can be saved except the conditions I have described.

Believe in God. Believe that he exists and that he created all things, both in heaven and on earth. Believe that he has all wisdom and all power, both in heaven and on earth. Believe that man does not comprehend everything the Lord can comprehend.

And also believe that you must repent of your sins, forsake them, and humble yourselves before God. Ask in sincerity of heart that he would forgive you. Now, if you believe all these things, make sure you do them.

Again I say to you, as I said before: since you have come to know the glory of God, and if you have known his goodness and tasted his love, and have received a remission of your sins — which causes such exceedingly great joy in your souls — I would have you remember, and always keep in remembrance, the greatness of God and your own nothingness, and his goodness and long-suffering toward you, unworthy creatures. Humble yourselves even in the depths of humility, calling on the name of the Lord daily, standing firm in the faith of what is to come, which was spoken by the mouth of the angel.

And I tell you that if you do this, you will always rejoice and be filled with the love of God, and always keep a remission of your sins. You will grow in the knowledge of the glory of him who created you, and in the knowledge of what is just and true.
You will not want to hurt one another but will live peacefully and give every person what they are owed.

You will not allow your children to go hungry or naked. You will not allow them to break the laws of God or fight and quarrel with one another and serve the devil, who is the master of sin — the evil spirit spoken of by our fathers and an enemy to all righteousness.
Instead, you will teach them to walk in the ways of truth and good judgment. You will teach them to love one another and to serve one another.
You yourselves will also help those who need your help. You will share what you have with those in need. You will not turn away the beggar who asks you for help, leaving him to die.
Perhaps you will say: The man brought his misery on himself, so I will hold back and not give him my food or share my possessions with him to ease his suffering, because his punishments are deserved.
But I say to you, whoever does this has great reason to repent. Unless he repents of what he has done, he will be lost forever and will have no place in the kingdom of God.
For aren't we all beggars? Don't we all depend on the same Being — God — for everything we have, for food and clothing, for gold, silver, and all the riches of every kind?

Even right now, you have been calling on his name and begging for a remission of your sins. And has he let you beg in vain? No — he has poured out his Spirit on you and caused your hearts to be filled with joy. He caused your mouths to be stopped because you could not speak, your joy was so great.

Now, if God, who created you and on whom you depend for your lives and everything you have and are, grants you whatever you ask that is right, when you ask in faith believing you will receive — then how much more should you share what you have with one another!

If you judge the person who begs you for help so he will not die, and you condemn him, how much more just will your own condemnation be? For you are withholding your possessions, which do not belong to you but to God, to whom your life also belongs. Yet you do not ask for help or repent of what you have done.
I say to you, woe to that person, for his possessions will perish with him. I say these things to those who are rich regarding the things of this world.

And again, I say to the poor — you who do not have much yet have enough to get by from day to day — I mean all of you who turn away the beggar because you have nothing: I would have you say in your hearts, 'I don't give because I have nothing, but if I had something I would give.'
If you say this in your hearts, you remain guiltless. Otherwise you are condemned, and your condemnation is just because you covet what you have not received.

For the sake of these things I have spoken — for the sake of keeping a remission of your sins from day to day, that you may walk guiltless before God — I would have you share what you have with the poor, every person according to what he has. Feed the hungry, clothe the naked, visit the sick, and help with their relief, both spiritually and physically, according to their needs.

And make sure all these things are done wisely and in order. No one is expected to run faster than he has strength. Yet it is important that he be diligent, so he might win the prize. Therefore all things must be done in order.
And I would have you remember that whoever borrows from his neighbor should return what he borrowed as he agreed, or else he commits sin. And perhaps he causes his neighbor to commit sin also.
And finally, I cannot tell you all the ways you can commit sin, for there are many different ways — so many that I cannot number them.

But this much I can tell you: if you do not watch yourselves, your thoughts, your words, and your deeds, and observe the commandments of God, and continue in the faith of what you have heard about the coming of our Lord, even to the end of your lives — you must perish. Now, remember this, and do not perish.

5.text: When King Benjamin had finished speaking to his people, he sent among them wanting to know if they believed the words he had spoken to them.

They all cried out with one voice, saying: Yes, we believe all the words you have spoken to us. We know they are sure and true because of the Spirit of the Lord Omnipotent, which has made a mighty change in us — in our hearts — so that we no longer want to do evil but want to do good continually.
We ourselves, through the infinite goodness of God and the manifestations of his Spirit, have great views of what is to come. If it were appropriate, we could prophesy of all things.
It is the faith we have had in the things our king has spoken to us that has brought us to this great knowledge, and we rejoice with exceedingly great joy.

We are willing to enter into a covenant with our God to do his will and to be obedient to his commandments in all things he commands us for the rest of our days, so we may not bring upon ourselves never-ending torment, as the angel has spoken, and so we may not drink from the cup of God's wrath.
These are the words King Benjamin wanted from them. Therefore he said to them: You have spoken the words I desired, and the covenant you have made is a righteous covenant.

Now, because of the covenant you have made, you will be called the children of Christ, his sons and his daughters. For today he has spiritually given you birth, because you say your hearts are changed through faith in his name. Therefore you are born of him and have become his sons and daughters.

Under this name you are made free, and there is no other name by which you can be made free. There is no other name given by which salvation comes. Therefore I would have you take upon you the name of Christ — all of you who have entered into the covenant with God to be obedient until the end of your lives.
Whoever does this will be found at the right hand of God, for he will know the name by which he is called — for he will be called by the name of Christ.
Whoever does not take upon him the name of Christ must be called by some other name. Therefore he finds himself on the left hand of God.

I want you to remember also that this is the name I said I would give you, one that would never be blotted out except through transgression. Therefore, take care that you do not transgress, so the name will not be blotted out of your hearts.

I say to you, I want you to remember to always keep the name written in your hearts, so you are not found on the left hand of God, but so you hear and know the voice by which you will be called, and also the name by which he will call you.
For how does a person know the master he has not served, who is a stranger to him, and who is far from the thoughts and intents of his heart?

Does a man take a donkey that belongs to his neighbor and keep it? I say to you, no — he will not even let it feed among his flocks. He will drive it away and cast it out. I say to you, this is how it will be among you if you do not know the name by which you are called.

Therefore, I want you to be steadfast and immovable, always abounding in good works, so that Christ, the Lord God Omnipotent, may seal you as his own. May you be brought to heaven and have everlasting salvation and eternal life, through the wisdom, power, justice, and mercy of him who created all things in heaven and on earth, who is God above all. Amen.

6.text: After King Benjamin finished speaking to the people, he thought it was important to record the names of all those who had entered into a covenant with God to keep his commandments.
There was not one soul, except the little children, who had not entered into the covenant and taken upon them the name of Christ.

When King Benjamin had finished all these things and had consecrated his son Mosiah to be a ruler and king over his people, and had given him all the instructions concerning the kingdom, and had also appointed priests to teach the people — so they might hear and know the commandments of God and be stirred up in remembrance of the oath they had made — he dismissed the multitude. They all returned, each one according to their families, to their own homes.
Mosiah began to reign in his father's place. He began to reign when he was thirty years old, making a total of about four hundred and seventy-six years from the time Lehi left Jerusalem.
King Benjamin lived three more years and then he died.
King Mosiah walked in the ways of the Lord. He observed his judgments and statutes and kept his commandments in everything the Lord commanded him.

King Mosiah had his people work the land. He himself also worked the land, so he would not be a burden to his people, and so he could follow his father's example in all things. There was no conflict among his people for three years.

7.text: After King Mosiah had enjoyed continual peace for three years, he wanted to know about the people who had gone to live in the land of Lehi-Nephi, because his people had heard nothing from them since they left Zarahemla. The people kept pestering him about it.
King Mosiah allowed sixteen of their strong men to go to the land of Lehi-Nephi to find out about their brothers.
The next day they set out. Among them was a man named Ammon, a strong and mighty man and a descendant of Zarahemla. He was also their leader.
They did not know the route through the wilderness to get to the land of Lehi-Nephi, so they wandered for many days — forty days in all.
After wandering forty days, they came to a hill north of the land of Shilom, and there they set up their tents.
Ammon took three of his brothers — Amaleki, Helem, and Hem — and they went down into the land of Nephi.
They met the king of the people living in the land of Nephi and Shilom. They were surrounded by the king's guard, captured, bound, and thrown in prison.

After two days in prison, they were brought before the king again. Their bonds were loosened, and they stood before the king. They were permitted — or rather commanded — to answer whatever questions he asked them.

He said to them: I am Limhi, the son of Noah, who was the son of Zeniff, who came up from the land of Zarahemla to inherit this land — the land of their fathers. I was made king by the voice of the people.
Now I want to know why you were bold enough to come near the walls of the city when I myself was outside the gate with my guards.
The only reason I let you live is so I could question you. Otherwise I would have had my guards put you to death. You may speak.

When Ammon saw he was allowed to speak, he stepped forward and bowed before the king. Rising again, he said: O king, I am very thankful before God today that I am still alive and permitted to speak. I will try to speak boldly.

For I am sure that if you had known who I was, you would not have let me wear these chains. I am Ammon, a descendant of Zarahemla, and I have come from the land of Zarahemla to ask about our brothers whom Zeniff brought up from that land.

When Limhi heard Ammon's words, he was overjoyed and said: Now I know for sure that my brothers in the land of Zarahemla are still alive! I will rejoice, and tomorrow I will have my people rejoice too!

For we are in bondage to the Lamanites. We are taxed with a tax that is terrible to bear. Now our brothers will deliver us from our bondage, or from the Lamanites. We will be their slaves, for it is better to be slaves to the Nephites than to pay tribute to the king of the Lamanites.

King Limhi commanded his guards to stop binding Ammon and his brothers. He sent them to the hill north of Shilom to bring the rest of their group into the city so they could eat, drink, and rest from their journey, because they had suffered greatly from hunger, thirst, and exhaustion.
The next day, King Limhi sent a proclamation among all his people to gather at the temple to hear him speak.

When they gathered together, he spoke to them, saying: O my people, lift up your heads and be comforted! The time is near when we will no longer be subject to our enemies. Despite our many struggles, which have been unsuccessful, I believe there is still an effective fight to be made.

Therefore lift up your heads, rejoice, and put your trust in God — the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the God who brought the children of Israel out of Egypt, who caused them to walk through the Red Sea on dry ground and fed them with manna so they would not perish in the wilderness. He did many more things for them.
That same God brought our fathers out of Jerusalem and has kept and preserved his people until now. It is because of our wickedness and abominations that he has brought us into bondage.

You are all witnesses today that Zeniff, who was made king over this people, was overly eager to inherit the land of his fathers. He was therefore deceived by the cunning and craftiness of King Laman, who made a treaty with King Zeniff and gave him possession of part of the land — the city of Lehi-Nephi, the city of Shilom, and the surrounding land.

He did all this for the sole purpose of bringing this people into bondage. And now, at this time, we pay tribute to the king of the Lamanites amounting to half of our corn, barley, and all our grain of every kind, half the increase of our flocks and herds — even half of all we have or possess the king of the Lamanites demands from us, or our lives.
Isn't this terrible to bear? Isn't our affliction great? How much reason we have to mourn!
Yes, I tell you, we have great reasons to mourn. See how many of our brothers have been killed, their blood spilled in vain — all because of wickedness.

If this people had not fallen into transgression, the Lord would not have allowed this great evil to come upon them. But they would not listen to his words. Conflicts arose among them, so much that they shed blood among themselves.
They killed a prophet of the Lord — a chosen man of God who told them of their wickedness and abominations and prophesied of many things to come, including the coming of Christ.

Because he said that Christ was God, the Father of all things, and that he would take upon himself the image of man — the image after which man was created in the beginning — in other words, he said man was created in the image of God, and that God would come down among the children of men and take on flesh and blood and walk on the earth —
Because he said this, they put him to death. They did many other things that brought God's wrath upon them. So who is surprised they are in bondage and suffering severe afflictions?
For the Lord has said: I will not help my people in the day of their transgression. I will block their ways so they do not prosper, and their actions will be a stumbling block before them.
And again he says: If my people sow filthiness, they will reap the chaff of it in the whirlwind, and its effect is poison.
And again he says: If my people sow filthiness, they will reap the east wind, which brings immediate destruction.
Now, the promise of the Lord is fulfilled, and you are struck down and afflicted.

But if you turn to the Lord with full purpose of heart, put your trust in him, and serve him with all diligence of mind — if you do this, he will, according to his own will and pleasure, deliver you from bondage.

8.text: After King Limhi finished speaking to his people — for he said many things, and only a few of them are written in this book — he told his people everything about their brothers in the land of Zarahemla.
He had Ammon stand before the multitude and tell them everything that had happened to their brothers from the time Zeniff left the land until the time Ammon himself came.
Ammon also told them the last words King Benjamin had taught and explained them to the people of King Limhi so they could understand everything he said.
After he had done all this, King Limhi dismissed the multitude and had everyone return to their own home.
He had the plates containing the record of his people from the time they left Zarahemla brought before Ammon so he could read them.
As soon as Ammon had read the record, the king asked if he could interpret languages. Ammon told him he could not.

The king said: Being troubled by the suffering of my people, I sent forty-three of my people on a journey into the wilderness to find the land of Zarahemla so we could ask our brothers to deliver us from bondage.

They were lost in the wilderness for many days but were persistent. They did not find Zarahemla but returned to this land. They had traveled through a land with many waters and discovered a land covered with the bones of men and animals, and also covered with the ruins of all kinds of buildings. They had discovered a land that had been populated by a people as numerous as the hosts of Israel.
As proof that what they said is true, they brought twenty-four plates filled with engravings, and the plates are made of pure gold.
They also brought breastplates, which are large and made of brass and copper, and are perfectly preserved.

They also brought swords whose handles had rotted away, and the blades were corroded with rust. There is no one in the land who can interpret the language or engravings on the plates. So I asked you: Can you translate?

I ask you again: Do you know of anyone who can translate? I want these records translated into our language. Perhaps they will tell us about the remnant of the people who were destroyed — where these records came from. Perhaps they will tell us about this very people who were destroyed. I want to know the cause of their destruction.

Ammon said to him: I can assuredly tell you, O king, of a man who can translate the records. He has a device that he can look into and translate all ancient records. It is a gift from God. The devices are called interpreters, and no one can look into them unless he is commanded to, or he would be destroyed. Whoever is commanded to look into them is called a seer.
The king of the people in the land of Zarahemla is the man who is commanded to do these things and who has this high gift from God.
The king said that a seer is greater than a prophet.
Ammon said that a seer is a revelator and a prophet also. No person can have a greater gift unless he possesses the power of God, which no man can. Yet a man may receive great power from God.

A seer can know about things past and things to come. Through them, all things will be revealed — secret things will be made known, hidden things will come to light, and things that are otherwise unknown will be made known through them.
God has provided a way for people to work mighty miracles through faith. Therefore a seer becomes a great benefit to his fellow beings.

When Ammon finished speaking, the king was overjoyed and gave thanks to God, saying: Surely a great mystery is contained in these plates. These interpreters were surely prepared to reveal all such mysteries to the children of men.

O how marvelous are the works of the Lord, and how long he is patient with his people! How blind and unperceptive are the minds of the children of men, for they will not seek wisdom, nor do they want it to rule over them!
They are like a wild flock that runs from the shepherd, scatters, and is driven and devoured by the beasts of the forest.

9.text: I, Zeniff, was taught in all the language of the Nephites and knew about the land of Nephi, the land of our fathers' first inheritance. I was sent as a spy among the Lamanites to scout their forces so our army could attack and destroy them. But when I saw the good among them, I did not want them destroyed.

So I argued with my fellow soldiers in the wilderness, wanting our leader to make a treaty with them. But he was a harsh and bloodthirsty man and ordered my death. I was saved, but only after much blood was shed — for father fought against father and brother against brother until most of our army was destroyed in the wilderness. Those of us who survived returned to Zarahemla to tell the tale to the wives and children.

Yet, being overly eager to inherit the land of our fathers, I gathered as many as wanted to go up and possess the land, and we started our journey into the wilderness again. But we were struck with famine and severe afflictions because we were slow to remember the Lord our God.
Nevertheless, after many days of wandering in the wilderness, we set up our tents near the place where our brothers had been killed, which was close to the land of our fathers.
I went again with four of my men into the city to meet the king, to learn his intentions and whether I could go in with my people and possess the land in peace.
I went to the king, and he made a covenant with me that I could possess the land of Lehi-Nephi and the land of Shilom.
He also commanded his people to leave the land, and my people and I went in to possess it.
We began building structures and repairing the walls of the city — the walls of Lehi-Nephi and the city of Shilom.
We began working the ground, planting all kinds of seeds — corn, wheat, barley, neas, sheum, and all kinds of fruit. We began to multiply and prosper in the land.
It was the cunning and craftiness of King Laman that he gave up the land for us to possess — his plan was to bring my people into bondage.
After we had lived in the land for twelve years, King Laman grew uneasy, fearing that my people might become too strong in the land and that they could not overpower and enslave them.
The Lamanites were a lazy and idol-worshipping people. They wanted to enslave us so they could live off the labor of our hands and feast on the flocks from our fields.
So King Laman began to stir up his people to fight against my people, and wars and conflicts began in the land.

In the thirteenth year of my reign in the land of Nephi, south of the land of Shilom, while my people were watering and feeding their flocks and working their land, a large Lamanite army attacked them, killing some and taking their flocks and the corn from their fields.
Everyone who was not overtaken fled to the city of Nephi and called on me for protection.
I armed them with bows, arrows, swords, curved blades, clubs, slings, and every weapon we could make. Then my people and I went out to battle against the Lamanites.

In the strength of the Lord we went to battle against the Lamanites. My people and I cried mightily to the Lord to deliver us from our enemies, for we were stirred up to remember how our fathers had been delivered.
God heard our cries and answered our prayers. We went out in his power. In one day and night we killed three thousand and forty-three of them, driving them out of our land.
I myself helped bury their dead with my own hands. To our great sorrow, two hundred and seventy-nine of our own brothers were killed.

10.text: We began to reestablish the kingdom and possess the land in peace again. I had weapons of war made of every kind so my people would be prepared when the Lamanites came to attack again.
I set guards around the land so the Lamanites could not surprise and destroy us again. This is how I protected my people and flocks, keeping them from falling into the hands of our enemies.
We inherited the land of our fathers for many years — twenty-two years.
I had the men work the ground and raise all kinds of grain and fruit of every kind.
I had the women spin, work hard, and make all kinds of fine linen and cloth of every kind to clothe ourselves. We prospered in the land and had continual peace for twenty-two years.
Then King Laman died, and his son began to reign in his place. He started stirring up his people to rebel against my people, so they began preparing for war and coming to battle against us.
I had sent spies throughout the land of Shemlon to discover their war preparations so I could guard against them and prevent them from surprising and destroying my people.

They came from the north of the land of Shilom with a huge army of men armed with bows, arrows, swords, curved blades, stones, and slings. Their heads were shaved so they were bare, and they wore leather belts around their waists.

I had the women and children hidden in the wilderness. I also gathered all my old men who could still bear arms and all my young men who were able to fight, and I arranged them in their ranks, each man according to his age.
We went to battle against the Lamanites. Even I, in my old age, went to fight. We went in the strength of the Lord.
The Lamanites knew nothing about the Lord or the strength of the Lord, so they relied on their own strength. Yet they were a strong people in physical strength.

They were a wild, fierce, and bloodthirsty people who believed the traditions of their fathers, which said they had been driven out of Jerusalem because of the wickedness of their fathers, and they were wronged by their brothers in the wilderness, and wronged again while crossing the sea.

They also believed they were wronged in the land of their first inheritance after crossing the sea — all because Nephi was more faithful in keeping the Lord's commandments. Therefore the Lord favored him, heard his prayers, answered them, and let him lead their journey through the wilderness.
His brothers were angry with him because they did not understand the Lord's dealings. They were also angry with him on the waters because they hardened their hearts against the Lord.
They were angry with him again when they arrived in the promised land because they said he had taken the ruling of the people from their hands, and they tried to kill him.
They were angry with him again because he left for the wilderness as the Lord commanded and took the records engraved on the brass plates. They said he robbed them.

This is what they taught their children — to hate the Nephites, murder them, rob and plunder them, and do everything possible to destroy them. Therefore the Lamanites have an eternal hatred toward the children of Nephi.

For this very reason, King Laman, through his cunning, lies, and false promises, deceived me so that I brought my people to this land where they could destroy them. We have suffered many years in the land.
After telling my people all these things about the Lamanites, I encouraged them to go to battle with all their might, putting their trust in the Lord. So we fought them face to face.
We drove them out of our land again. We killed so many that we did not even count them.
We returned to our land, and my people began tending their flocks and working their ground again.
Being old, I gave the kingdom to one of my sons. Therefore I say no more. May the Lord bless my people. Amen.

11.text: Zeniff gave the kingdom to Noah, one of his sons. So Noah began to reign in his place, but he did not walk in the ways of his father.

He did not keep the commandments of God but followed the desires of his own heart. He had many wives and concubines. He caused his people to commit sin and do what was abominable in the sight of the Lord. They committed terrible immorality and all kinds of wickedness.
He imposed a tax of one-fifth of everything they owned — a fifth of their gold, silver, ziff, copper, brass, and iron; a fifth of their fattened animals; and a fifth of all their grain.
He took all this to support himself, his wives and concubines, and also his priests and their wives and concubines. This is how he changed the affairs of the kingdom.
He removed all the priests his father had consecrated and put new ones in their place — ones who were lifted up in the pride of their hearts.
They were supported in their laziness, idolatry, and immorality by the taxes King Noah imposed on his people. The people labored excessively to support wickedness.
The people also became idol worshippers because they were deceived by the empty and flattering words of the king and priests, who spoke flattering things to them.
King Noah built many elegant and spacious buildings. He decorated them with fine woodwork and all kinds of precious things — gold, silver, iron, brass, ziff, and copper.
He also built a spacious palace with a throne in the middle of it, all made of fine wood and decorated with gold, silver, and precious things.
He also had his workers create all kinds of fine craftsmanship on the walls of the temple — fine wood, copper, and brass.

The seats set apart for the high priests, above all the other seats, he decorated with pure gold. He had a railing built in front of them so they could rest their bodies and arms while they spoke lying and vain words to his people.

He built a tower near the temple — a very high tower. He could stand on top of it and overlook the land of Shilom and the land of Shemlon, which the Lamanites possessed. He could even see all the land around.

He had many buildings constructed in the land of Shilom and built a great tower on the hill north of Shilom, which had been a place of refuge for the Nephites when they fled. He did all this with the riches he took from taxing his people.
He set his heart on his riches and spent his time in reckless living with his wives and concubines. His priests also spent their time with immoral women.
He planted vineyards throughout the land, built winepresses, and made wine in abundance. He became a heavy drinker, and so did his people.
The Lamanites began attacking his people in small numbers, killing them in their fields and while they were tending their flocks.

King Noah sent guards throughout the land to keep them away, but he did not send enough. The Lamanites attacked, killed them, and drove off many of their flocks. The Lamanites began to destroy them and pour out their hatred on them.
King Noah sent his armies against them and drove them back for a time. So they returned celebrating their plunder.

Because of this great victory, they became prideful. They boasted in their own strength, saying their fifty could stand against thousands of Lamanites. They boasted and delighted in blood and the killing of their brothers — all because of the wickedness of their king and priests.

There was a man among them named Abinadi. He went among them and began to prophesy, saying: The Lord has said and commanded me: Go and say to this people, 'The Lord says: Woe to this people, for I have seen their abominations, their wickedness, and their immorality. Unless they repent, I will visit them in my anger.'
Unless they repent and turn to the Lord their God, I will deliver them into the hands of their enemies. They will be brought into bondage and afflicted by their enemies.
They will know that I am the Lord their God — a God who demands faithfulness, and who holds his people accountable for their sins.
Unless this people repents and turns to the Lord their God, they will be brought into bondage, and no one will deliver them except the Lord Almighty God.
When they cry out to me, I will be slow to hear their cries. I will allow them to be defeated by their enemies.

Unless they repent in deep humility and cry mightily to the Lord their God, I will not hear their prayers or deliver them from their afflictions. This is what the Lord says, and this is what he has commanded me.
When Abinadi had spoken these words to them, they were angry and wanted to take his life. But the Lord delivered him from their hands.

When King Noah heard what Abinadi had told the people, he was also angry. He said: Who is Abinadi, that I and my people should be judged by him? And who is the Lord that would bring such great affliction on my people?
I command you to bring Abinadi here so I can kill him, for he has said these things to stir up my people to anger against one another and cause conflicts among them. Therefore I will kill him.

The eyes of the people were blinded. They hardened their hearts against Abinadi's words and tried to capture him from then on. King Noah also hardened his heart against the word of the Lord and did not repent of his evil doings.

12.text: After two years, Abinadi came among them in disguise so they did not recognize him. He began to prophesy, saying: The Lord has commanded me, saying — Abinadi, go and prophesy to my people, for they have hardened their hearts against my words. They have not repented of their evil doings. Therefore I will visit them in my anger — in my fierce anger I will punish them for their sins and abominations.

Woe to this generation! The Lord said to me: Stretch out your hand and prophesy, saying — The Lord says: This generation, because of their sins, will be brought into bondage. They will be struck on the cheek, driven by men, and killed. The vultures, the dogs, and the wild beasts will devour their flesh.
The life of King Noah will be valued like a piece of clothing in a hot furnace, for he will know that I am the Lord.
I will strike my people with severe afflictions — with famine and pestilence — and I will cause them to cry out all day long.
I will cause heavy burdens to be lashed on their backs, and they will be driven like dumb donkeys.
I will send hail to strike them. They will also be struck by the east wind. Insects will plague their land and devour their grain.
They will be struck with a great disease. All this I will do because of their sins and abominations.

Unless they repent, I will utterly destroy them from the face of the earth. Yet they will leave a record behind, and I will preserve it for other nations that will possess the land. I will do this to reveal the abominations of this people to other nations. Abinadi prophesied many things against this people.

They were angry with him. They seized him, bound him, and brought him before the king, saying: We have brought a man before you who has prophesied evil against your people and says God will destroy them.
He also prophesies evil about your life, saying your life will be like a piece of clothing in a furnace of fire.
And again, he says you will be like a dry stalk in the field, trampled under foot by animals.

And again, he says you will be like the blossoms of a thistle, which, when fully ripe, the wind blows across the face of the land. He claims the Lord has spoken it. He says all this will come upon you unless you repent, and this because of your sins.
Now, O king, what great evil have you done, or what great sins have your people committed, that we should be condemned by God or judged by this man?
We are guiltless, and you, O king, have not sinned. Therefore this man has lied about you and prophesied in vain.
We are strong. We will not come into bondage or be captured by our enemies. You have prospered in the land, and you will also continue to prosper.
Here is the man. We deliver him to you. Do with him as you see fit.
King Noah had Abinadi thrown into prison. He commanded the priests to gather so he could hold a council about what to do with him.
They told the king: Bring him here so we can question him. The king commanded that he be brought before them.

They began to question him, trying to trip him up so they could have something to accuse him of. But he answered them boldly and withstood all their questions — to their astonishment. He stood his ground against all their questions and confounded them with every word.
One of them said to him: What do the words mean that are written and taught by our fathers, saying:
How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of the one who brings good news, who proclaims peace, who brings good news of good things, who proclaims salvation, who says to Zion, 'Your God reigns!'
Your watchmen will lift up their voices. Together they will sing, for they will see eye to eye when the Lord brings Zion back.
Break forth into joy! Sing together, you waste places of Jerusalem! For the Lord has comforted his people. He has redeemed Jerusalem.
The Lord has revealed his holy arm before all nations, and all the ends of the earth will see the salvation of our God.
Abinadi said to them: Are you priests who pretend to teach this people and to understand the spirit of prophecy, and yet you want me to tell you what these things mean?
I say to you, woe to you for twisting the ways of the Lord! If you understand these things, you have not taught them. Therefore you have twisted the ways of the Lord.
You have not applied your hearts to understanding. Therefore you have not been wise. So what do you teach this people?
They said: We teach the law of Moses.

He said to them: If you teach the law of Moses, why don't you keep it? Why do you set your hearts on riches? Why do you commit immorality and spend your energy with immoral women? You cause this people to sin, giving the Lord reason to send me to prophesy against this people — a great evil against this people.
Don't you know I speak the truth? Yes, you know I speak the truth, and you should tremble before God.
You will be punished for your sins. You say you teach the law of Moses. But what do you know about the law of Moses? Does salvation come by the law of Moses? What do you say?
They answered that salvation did come by the law of Moses.
But Abinadi said to them: I know that if you keep God's commandments you will be saved — the commandments the Lord gave Moses on Mount Sinai, saying:
I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage.
You shall have no other god before me.
You shall not make any carved image, or any likeness of anything in heaven above or on the earth beneath.
Now Abinadi said to them: Have you done all this? I say to you, no, you have not. And have you taught this people to do all these things? I say to you, no, you have not.

13.text: When the king heard these words, he told his priests: Take this fellow away and kill him. What do we have to do with him? He is mad.
They stepped forward and tried to lay their hands on him, but he resisted them and said:

Do not touch me, for God will strike you if you lay your hands on me. I have not yet delivered the message the Lord sent me to deliver, nor have I told you what you asked me to tell you. Therefore, God will not allow me to be destroyed at this time.
I must fulfill the commandments God has given me. Because I have told you the truth, you are angry with me. And because I have spoken the word of God, you have judged me to be mad.

After Abinadi said these words, the people of King Noah did not dare lay their hands on him, for the Spirit of the Lord was upon him. His face shone with exceeding brightness, just as Moses' face did on Mount Sinai while speaking with the Lord.
He spoke with power and authority from God and continued his words, saying:
You see that you do not have power to kill me. Therefore I will finish my message. I can see it cuts you to the heart because I tell you the truth about your sins.
My words fill you with wonder, amazement, and anger.
But I will finish my message. After that, it does not matter what happens to me, as long as I am saved.
But this much I tell you: what you do with me after this will be a type and shadow of things to come.
Now I will read to you the rest of God's commandments, for I can see they are not written in your hearts. I can see you have studied and taught wickedness for most of your lives.
Now remember, I said to you: You shall not make any carved image, or any likeness of anything in heaven above, on the earth beneath, or in the water under the earth.

And again: You shall not bow down to them or serve them, for I the Lord your God am a jealous God, holding the children accountable for the sins of the fathers to the third and fourth generations of those who hate me.
But showing mercy to thousands of those who love me and keep my commandments.
You shall not take the name of the Lord your God in vain, for the Lord will not hold him guiltless who takes his name in vain.
Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy.
Six days you shall labor and do all your work.

But the seventh day, the sabbath of the Lord your God, you shall not do any work — not you, nor your son, nor your daughter, your male servant or female servant, nor your cattle, nor the stranger within your gates.
For in six days the Lord made heaven and earth, the sea, and everything in them. Therefore the Lord blessed the sabbath day and made it holy.
Honor your father and mother, so your days may be long in the land the Lord your God gives you.
You shall not kill.
You shall not commit adultery. You shall not steal.
You shall not give false testimony against your neighbor.
You shall not covet your neighbor's house. You shall not covet your neighbor's wife, his male servant, his female servant, his ox, his donkey, or anything that belongs to your neighbor.
After Abinadi finished saying these things, he said: Have you taught this people to observe and do all these things to keep these commandments?
I say to you, no. If you had, the Lord would not have sent me to prophesy evil against this people.

Now you have said that salvation comes by the law of Moses. I say to you that it is still necessary to keep the law of Moses for now. But I tell you, the time will come when it will no longer be necessary to keep the law of Moses.

Moreover, I tell you that salvation does not come by the law alone. If it were not for the atonement that God himself will make for the sins and wickedness of his people, they would unavoidably perish, despite the law of Moses.
It was necessary that a law be given to the children of Israel — a very strict law. For they were a stubborn people, quick to do evil and slow to remember the Lord their God.
Therefore a law was given to them — a law of performances and ordinances that they were to observe strictly day by day, to keep them in remembrance of God and their duty to him.
But all these things were types of things to come.

Did they understand the law? I say no, they did not all understand it. This was because of the hardness of their hearts. They did not understand that no one can be saved except through the redemption of God.

Did not Moses prophesy to them about the coming of the Messiah, and that God would redeem his people? Yes, and every prophet who has prophesied since the world began — have they not spoken more or less about these things?
Have they not said that God himself would come down among the children of men, take on the form of a man, and go forth in mighty power on the earth?
Have they not also said that he would bring about the resurrection of the dead and that he himself would be oppressed and afflicted?

14.text: Does not Isaiah say: Who has believed our report, and to whom is the arm of the Lord revealed?
For he will grow up before him as a tender plant, as a root out of dry ground. He has no impressive form or beauty. When we see him, there is nothing attractive that we should desire him.
He is despised and rejected by men — a man of sorrows, familiar with grief. We hid our faces from him. He was despised, and we did not value him.
Surely he has carried our griefs and borne our sorrows. Yet we thought he was struck down, smitten by God, and afflicted.
But he was wounded for our transgressions, crushed for our sins. The punishment that brought us peace was upon him, and by his wounds we are healed.
All of us, like sheep, have gone astray. Each of us has turned to his own way. And the Lord has laid on him the sins of us all.
He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth. He is brought like a lamb to the slaughter, and like a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth.
He was taken from prison and from judgment. Who will declare his generation? For he was cut off from the land of the living. He was struck down for the transgressions of my people.
He was given a grave with the wicked, and with the rich in his death — because he had done no evil, and there was no deceit in his mouth.

Yet it pleased the Lord to crush him and put him to grief. When his soul is made an offering for sin, he will see his offspring, he will prolong his days, and the Lord's purpose will prosper in his hand.
He will see the result of his suffering and be satisfied. By his knowledge, my righteous servant will justify many, for he will bear their sins.

Therefore I will give him a portion with the great, and he will divide the spoil with the strong — because he poured out his soul unto death. He was numbered with the transgressors, bore the sins of many, and made intercession for the transgressors.

15.text: Abinadi said to them: I want you to understand that God himself will come down among the children of men and will redeem his people.
Because he dwells in a mortal body, he will be called the Son of God. Having subjected the flesh to the will of the Father, being the Father and the Son —
The Father, because he was conceived by the power of God; and the Son, because of the flesh — thus becoming the Father and Son.
They are one God — the very Eternal Father of heaven and earth.

And so the flesh becomes subject to the Spirit, or the Son to the Father, being one God. He endures temptation and does not yield to it, but allows himself to be mocked, whipped, cast out, and rejected by his people.
After all this, after performing many mighty miracles among the children of men, he will be led — just as Isaiah said — like a sheep before the shearer is silent, so he did not open his mouth.
Even so he will be led, crucified, and killed. The flesh becomes subject even to death, the will of the Son being swallowed up in the will of the Father.
And so God breaks the bands of death, having gained victory over death, giving the Son power to make intercession for the children of men.

Having ascended into heaven, having compassion and mercy, being filled with sympathy toward the children of men, standing between them and justice, having broken the bands of death, taken their sins and transgressions upon himself, redeemed them, and satisfied the demands of justice.
Now I say to you, who will declare his generation? When his soul has been made an offering for sin, he will see his offspring. Now what do you say? Who will be his offspring?

I tell you, whoever has heard the words of the prophets — all the holy prophets who prophesied about the coming of the Lord — I say to you, all those who listened to their words, believed the Lord would redeem his people, and looked forward to that day for a remission of their sins — these are his offspring, the heirs of the kingdom of God.
For these are the ones whose sins he has carried. These are the ones for whom he died, to redeem them from their transgressions. Are they not his offspring?
And are not the prophets — every one who has prophesied and not fallen into transgression, every holy prophet since the world began — are they not his offspring?
These are they who have proclaimed peace, who have brought good news of good things, who have proclaimed salvation, and said to Zion: Your God reigns!
O how beautiful on the mountains were their feet!
And again, how beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who are still proclaiming peace!
And again, how beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who will proclaim peace from now on and forever!

And I tell you, this is not all. For how beautiful on the mountains are the feet of the one who brings good news — the founder of peace, the Lord himself, who has redeemed his people and granted salvation to his people.
Without the redemption he has made for his people, which was prepared from the foundation of the world, all mankind would have perished.
But the bands of death will be broken. The Son reigns and has power over the dead. Therefore he brings about the resurrection of the dead.
There will be a resurrection — a first resurrection, a resurrection of those who have lived, who are living now, and who will live, even until the resurrection of Christ — for so he will be called.

The resurrection of all the prophets, and all those who believed their words, and all those who kept God's commandments, will come forth in the first resurrection. Therefore, they are the first resurrection.
They are raised to live with God who redeemed them. They have eternal life through Christ, who broke the bands of death.

These are those who have part in the first resurrection — those who died before Christ came, in their ignorance, without having salvation declared to them. The Lord brings about their restoration, and they have part in the first resurrection, having eternal life, being redeemed by the Lord.
And little children also have eternal life.

But be afraid and tremble before God, for you should tremble. The Lord does not redeem anyone who rebels against him and dies in their sins. All those throughout all time who have perished in their sins, who willfully rebelled against God, who knew God's commandments and would not keep them — they have no part in the first resurrection.
Therefore shouldn't you tremble? Salvation comes to none of them. The Lord has not redeemed them, nor can he, for he cannot deny himself. He cannot deny justice when it has its claim.
The time will come when the salvation of the Lord will be declared to every nation, family, language, and people.
Lord, your watchmen will lift up their voices. Together they will sing, for they will see eye to eye when the Lord brings Zion back.
Break forth into joy! Sing together, you waste places of Jerusalem, for the Lord has comforted his people. He has redeemed Jerusalem.
The Lord has revealed his holy arm before all nations, and all the ends of the earth will see the salvation of our God.

16.text: After Abinadi finished these words, he stretched out his hand and said: The time will come when all will see the salvation of the Lord — when every nation, family, language, and people will see eye to eye and confess before God that his judgments are just.
Then the wicked will be cast out. They will have cause to howl, weep, wail, and gnash their teeth — because they would not listen to the voice of the Lord. Therefore the Lord does not redeem them.

For they are carnal and devilish. The devil has power over them — that old serpent who deceived our first parents, which caused their fall. This caused all mankind to become carnal, sensual, and devilish, knowing evil from good and subjecting themselves to the devil.
All mankind was lost. And they would have been endlessly lost if God had not redeemed his people from their lost and fallen state.

But remember, whoever persists in his own sinful nature and continues in sin and rebellion against God remains in his fallen state. The devil has all power over him. He is as though there was no redemption made, being an enemy to God. The devil is also an enemy to God.
If Christ had not come into the world, speaking of future things as though they had already happened, there could have been no redemption.
If Christ had not risen from the dead, or broken the bands of death so the grave has no victory and death has no sting, there could have been no resurrection.
But there is a resurrection. Therefore the grave has no victory, and the sting of death is swallowed up in Christ.
He is the light and life of the world — a light that is endless, that can never be darkened; and a life that is endless, where there can be no more death.

Even this mortal body will put on immortality, and this corruption will put on incorruption. Everyone will be brought before the bar of God to be judged according to their works — whether good or evil.

If their works are good, they are resurrected to endless life and happiness. If their works are evil, they are resurrected to endless damnation, being given over to the devil who subjected them — which is damnation.

They went according to their own sinful desires. They never called upon the Lord while the arms of mercy were extended toward them. For the arms of mercy were extended toward them, and they would not accept. They were warned of their sins and yet would not leave them. They were commanded to repent and yet would not repent.
Now shouldn't you tremble and repent of your sins, and remember that only in and through Christ can you be saved?
Therefore, if you teach the law of Moses, also teach that it is a shadow of things to come.
Teach them that redemption comes through Christ the Lord, who is the very Eternal Father. Amen.

17.text: When Abinadi had finished these words, the king commanded the priests to take him and have him put to death.

But there was one among them named Alma, also a descendant of Nephi. He was a young man, and he believed the words Abinadi had spoken, for he knew about the wickedness Abinadi testified against. He began to plead with the king not to be angry with Abinadi but to let him go in peace.
But the king was even more angry. He had Alma cast out and sent servants after him to kill him.
Alma fled and hid so they could not find him. While in hiding for many days, he wrote down all the words Abinadi had spoken.
The king had his guards surround Abinadi and take him. They bound him and threw him into prison.
After three days, having consulted with his priests, the king had Abinadi brought before him again.
He said to him: Abinadi, we have found a charge against you, and you deserve death.
For you said that God himself would come down among the children of men. For this reason you will be put to death, unless you take back everything you have said against me and my people.
Abinadi said: I say to you, I will not take back the words I have spoken about this people, for they are true. So you may know they are certain, I have allowed myself to fall into your hands.

I will suffer even death, and I will not take back my words. They will stand as a testimony against you. If you kill me, you will shed innocent blood, and this will also stand as a testimony against you at the last day.
King Noah was about to release him, because he feared Abinadi's words and feared that God's judgments would come upon him.
But the priests raised their voices against Abinadi and accused him, saying: He has insulted the king. This stirred up the king's anger, and he handed Abinadi over to be killed.
They took him, bound him, and burned his skin with torches, even to the point of death.
When the flames began to scorch him, he cried out to them, saying:

Just as you have done to me, so it will come to pass that your descendants will cause many to suffer the pains I now suffer — the pains of death by fire — because they believe in the salvation of the Lord their God.
You will be afflicted with all kinds of diseases because of your sins.
You will be struck on every side, driven, and scattered like a wild flock chased by wild and ferocious beasts.
In that day you will be hunted and captured by your enemies. Then you will suffer, as I suffer, the pains of death by fire.
God executes vengeance on those who destroy his people. O God, receive my soul.
When Abinadi said these words, he fell, having died by fire. He was put to death because he would not deny the commandments of God, having sealed the truth of his words by his death.

18.text: Alma, who had fled from King Noah's servants, repented of his sins and went privately among the people, teaching the words of Abinadi.
He taught about what was to come — the resurrection of the dead, the redemption of the people through the power, suffering, death, resurrection, and ascension of Christ into heaven.
He taught all who would listen. He taught them privately so the king would not find out. Many believed his words.
Those who believed went to a place called Mormon. It got its name from the king and was on the border of the land. At times it was infested with wild beasts.
In Mormon there was a spring of pure water. Alma went there often. Near the water was a grove of small trees where he hid during the day from the king's searches.
Everyone who believed him came there to hear his words.

After many days, a good number had gathered at the place of Mormon to hear Alma. Everyone who believed in his word gathered to hear him. He taught and preached to them about repentance, redemption, and faith in the Lord.

He said to them: Here are the waters of Mormon — for that is what they are called. Now, since you want to come into the fold of God and be called his people, and are willing to bear one another's burdens so they may be light —

and are willing to mourn with those who mourn, comfort those who need comfort, and stand as witnesses of God at all times and in all things and in all places, even until death, so that you may be redeemed by God and be numbered with those of the first resurrection and have eternal life —

if this is the desire of your hearts, what do you have against being baptized in the name of the Lord, as a witness before him that you have entered into a covenant with him to serve him and keep his commandments, so that he may pour out his Spirit more abundantly upon you?
When the people heard these words, they clapped their hands for joy and exclaimed: This is the desire of our hearts!
