Alma took Helam, one of the first, and went into the water. He cried out, saying: O Lord, pour out your Spirit upon your servant, that he may do this work with holiness of heart.

When he said these words, the Spirit of the Lord was upon him, and he said: Helam, I baptize you, having authority from the Almighty God, as a testimony that you have entered into a covenant to serve him until you are dead as to the mortal body. May the Spirit of the Lord be poured out upon you, and may he grant you eternal life through the redemption of Christ, whom he has prepared from the foundation of the world.
After Alma said these words, both Alma and Helam were buried in the water. They arose and came out of the water rejoicing, being filled with the Spirit.
Alma took another person and went into the water a second time and baptized him in the same manner as the first, only this time Alma did not bury himself in the water again.
In this way he baptized everyone who came to the place of Mormon. They numbered about two hundred and four souls. They were baptized in the waters of Mormon and were filled with the grace of God.
From that time on, they were called the church of God, or the church of Christ. Whoever was baptized by the power and authority of God was added to his church.
Alma, having authority from God, ordained priests — one priest for every fifty people — to preach and teach them about the things pertaining to the kingdom of God.
He commanded them to teach nothing except what he had taught and what had been spoken by the holy prophets.
He commanded them to preach nothing but repentance and faith in the Lord who had redeemed his people.
He commanded them that there should be no arguing with one another. They should look forward with one focus, having one faith and one baptism, with their hearts knit together in unity and love.
This is what he commanded them to preach. And so they became the children of God.
He commanded them to observe the sabbath day and keep it holy, and also to give thanks to the Lord their God every day.
He also commanded that the priests he had ordained should work with their own hands to support themselves.
One day every week was set apart for them to gather together to teach the people and worship the Lord their God, and to assemble as often as they could.

The priests were not to depend on the people for support. For their labor, they were to receive the grace of God, so they might grow strong in the Spirit and gain the knowledge of God, and teach with power and authority from God.

Alma also commanded that the people of the church should share of what they had — everyone according to what he possessed. Whoever had more should share more, and whoever had little should have little required of him. To those who had nothing, something should be given.
They should share freely from their own good will and desire toward God, and give to those priests who were in need, and to every needy and destitute soul.
He said this to them as commanded by God. They walked uprightly before God, sharing with one another both temporally and spiritually according to their needs and wants.

All this was done in Mormon — by the waters of Mormon, in the forest near the waters of Mormon. The place of Mormon, the waters of Mormon, the forest of Mormon — how beautiful they are to those who came there to know their Redeemer! How blessed they are, for they will sing to his praise forever.
These things were done in the outskirts of the land so they would not come to the knowledge of the king.
But the king discovered a movement among the people. He sent servants to spy on them. On the day they were assembling to hear the word of the Lord, they were discovered.
The king said Alma was stirring the people to rebellion against him. He sent his army to destroy them.
Alma and the people of the Lord were warned about the coming army. They took their tents and families and fled into the wilderness.
They numbered about four hundred and fifty souls.

19.text: The king's army returned, having searched in vain for the people of the Lord.
Now the king's forces were small, having been reduced, and a division began among the remaining people.
The minority began to threaten the king, and great conflict arose among them.
There was a man among them named Gideon, a strong man and enemy of the king. He drew his sword and swore in his anger that he would kill the king.
He fought with the king, and when the king saw he was about to be overpowered, he fled and ran up the tower near the temple.
Gideon chased him and was about to climb the tower to kill the king when the king looked out toward the land of Shemlon and saw the Lamanite army approaching their borders.
The king cried out in anguish: Gideon, spare me! The Lamanites are upon us and will destroy us — they will destroy my people!
The king was not so concerned about his people as about his own life. Nevertheless, Gideon spared his life.
The king ordered the people to flee from the Lamanites. He himself went ahead of them, and they fled into the wilderness with their women and children.
The Lamanites pursued them, overtook them, and began to kill them.
The king commanded all the men to leave their wives and children and flee from the Lamanites.
Many would not leave them, choosing rather to stay and die with them. The rest left their wives and children and fled.
Those who stayed with their wives and children had their beautiful daughters stand before the Lamanites and plead for their lives.
The Lamanites had compassion on them, for they were charmed by the beauty of their women.

Therefore the Lamanites spared their lives and took them captive. They brought them back to the land of Nephi and allowed them to possess the land, on the condition that they deliver King Noah to the Lamanites and give up half of everything they owned — half their gold, silver, and precious things. They would pay this tribute to the Lamanite king every year.
One of the king's sons, named Limhi, was among the captives.
Limhi did not want his father destroyed. But Limhi was not ignorant of his father's sins. Limhi himself was a just man.
Gideon sent men secretly into the wilderness to search for the king and those with him. They found the people in the wilderness — all except the king and his priests.
The people had sworn in their hearts to return to the land of Nephi. If their wives and children had been killed, along with those who stayed behind, they would seek revenge and die with them.
The king commanded them not to return, but they were angry with the king and caused him to suffer death by fire.
They were about to put the priests to death as well, but the priests fled from them.

As they were about to return to the land of Nephi, they met Gideon's men. The men told them everything that had happened to their wives and children — that the Lamanites had allowed them to possess the land in exchange for a tribute of half of everything they owned.
The people told Gideon's men they had killed the king and that his priests had fled further into the wilderness.
After they finished their proceedings, they returned to the land of Nephi, rejoicing because their wives and children had not been killed. They told Gideon what they had done to the king.
The king of the Lamanites made an oath that his people would not kill them.
Limhi, being the son of the king and having the kingdom given to him by the people, made an oath to the Lamanite king that his people would pay tribute — half of everything they owned.
Limhi began to establish the kingdom and establish peace among his people.
The Lamanite king set guards around the land to keep Limhi's people from leaving for the wilderness. He paid his guards with the tribute he received from the Nephites.
King Limhi had continual peace in his kingdom for two years, during which the Lamanites did not bother or try to destroy them.

20.text: There was a place in Shemlon where the daughters of the Lamanites gathered to sing, dance, and be merry.
One day, a small number of them gathered to sing and dance.
The priests of King Noah, who were ashamed to return to the city of Nephi and afraid the people would kill them, did not dare return to their wives and children.
Having stayed in the wilderness and having discovered the daughters of the Lamanites, they hid and watched them.
When only a few had gathered to dance, the priests came from their hiding places, seized them, and carried them into the wilderness — twenty-four of the Lamanite daughters.
When the Lamanites found their daughters missing, they were angry with the people of Limhi, thinking they had taken them.
They sent their armies — the king himself went ahead — and went to the land of Nephi to destroy Limhi's people.
Limhi spotted them from the tower. He saw all their war preparations. He gathered his people and set ambushes in the fields and forests.
When the Lamanites came, Limhi's people began attacking them from their hiding places and killing them.
The battle became extremely fierce. They fought like lions for their prey.

Limhi's people began driving the Lamanites before them, even though they were not half as numerous. But they fought for their lives, their wives, and their children. They fought with everything they had, like dragons.
They found the Lamanite king among the dead. He was not dead, but had been wounded and left on the ground — his people had fled so quickly they abandoned him.
They bound his wounds and brought him before Limhi, saying: Here is the king of the Lamanites. He was wounded and fell among the dead, and his people left him. Now let us kill him.

Limhi said: Do not kill him. Bring him to me. They brought him, and Limhi said: Why did you come to fight against my people? My people have not broken the oath I made to you. Why should you break the oath you made to my people?
The king said: I broke the oath because your people carried away the daughters of my people. In my anger, I sent my people to fight against yours.
Limhi had heard nothing about this. He said: I will search among my people, and whoever did this will die. So he ordered a search.
When Gideon, the king's captain, heard this, he went to the king and said: Please hold off. Do not search among the people or blame them for this.
Don't you remember the priests of your father, whom the people tried to destroy? Aren't they in the wilderness? Aren't they the ones who stole the daughters of the Lamanites?
Now, tell the king of the Lamanites about this so he can calm his people down toward us. Because right now they are already preparing to attack us again, and there are very few of us.
They are coming with their massive armies. Unless the king calms them, we will be destroyed.
Are not the words of Abinadi being fulfilled, which he prophesied against us? All this is because we would not listen to the words of the Lord and turn from our sins.
Now let us calm the king and fulfill the oath we made to him. It is better to be in bondage than to lose our lives. So let us stop the shedding of so much blood.
Limhi told the king everything about his father and the priests who had fled into the wilderness, and attributed the kidnapping of the daughters to them.
The king was calmed toward his people. He said: Let us go out to meet my people without weapons. I swear to you that my people will not kill your people.
They followed the king and went unarmed to meet the Lamanites. The Lamanite king bowed before them and pleaded on behalf of Limhi's people.
When the Lamanites saw Limhi's people unarmed, they had compassion and were calmed toward them. They returned with their king in peace to their own land.

21.text: Limhi and his people returned to the city of Nephi and began to live in the land again in peace.
After many days, the Lamanites began to be stirred up in anger against the Nephites and started coming into the borders of the land.

They did not dare kill them because of their king's oath to Limhi. But they would hit them on their cheeks, assert authority over them, put heavy burdens on their backs, and drive them like dumb donkeys.
All this was done so the word of the Lord might be fulfilled.
The afflictions of the Nephites were great. There was no way to free themselves because the Lamanites surrounded them on every side.

The people began to complain to the king about their afflictions. They wanted to go fight the Lamanites. They pressured the king so much with their complaints that he allowed them to do as they wished.
They gathered together, put on their armor, and went against the Lamanites to drive them out of the land.
The Lamanites beat them and drove them back, killing many of them.
There was great mourning and grief among Limhi's people — widows mourning their husbands, sons and daughters mourning their fathers, brothers mourning their brothers.
Now there were many widows in the land, and they cried out constantly because of their great fear of the Lamanites.
Their continual cries stirred the rest of Limhi's people to anger against the Lamanites. They went to battle again, but were driven back again, suffering heavy losses.
They went a third time and suffered the same result. Those not killed returned to the city of Nephi.
They humbled themselves even to the dust, submitting to the yoke of bondage — allowing themselves to be struck, driven around, and burdened according to the desires of their enemies.
They humbled themselves in the depths of humility and cried mightily to God all day long, begging him to deliver them from their afflictions.

The Lord was slow to hear their cries because of their sins. Nevertheless, the Lord did hear them and began to soften the hearts of the Lamanites so they eased their burdens. But the Lord did not yet see fit to deliver them from bondage.
They began to prosper gradually in the land and raise more grain and flocks and herds, so they did not suffer from hunger.
There were many more women than men because of the great number of men who had been killed. King Limhi commanded every man to help support the widows and their children so they would not starve.
Limhi's people stayed together as much as possible, keeping their grain and flocks secure.
The king himself did not go outside the city walls without his guards, fearing he might fall into Lamanite hands.

He had his people watch the surrounding land, hoping to catch the priests who had fled into the wilderness and stolen the Lamanite daughters, causing such destruction. They wanted to capture and punish them.
The priests had been sneaking into the land of Nephi at night, stealing grain and precious things. So Limhi's people set traps for them.
There was no more trouble between the Lamanites and Limhi's people until Ammon and his brothers arrived.

The king had been outside the city gates with his guard when he discovered Ammon and his brothers. Thinking they were priests of Noah, he had them captured, bound, and thrown in prison. If they had been Noah's priests, he would have had them killed.
When he found they were not priests but his brothers from Zarahemla, he was filled with exceedingly great joy.
Before Ammon came, King Limhi had sent a small group of men to find the land of Zarahemla, but they could not find it and got lost in the wilderness.

However, they found a land that had been populated — a land covered with dry bones, peopled and destroyed. Thinking it was Zarahemla, they returned to the land of Nephi, arriving just days before Ammon came.
They brought back a record — a record of the people whose bones they had found — engraved on plates of ore.
Limhi was again filled with joy when he learned from Ammon that King Mosiah had a gift from God to interpret such engravings. Ammon also rejoiced.
Yet Ammon and his brothers were filled with sorrow because so many of their brothers had been killed.

They also mourned that King Noah and his priests had caused the people to commit so many sins against God. They mourned for the death of Abinadi and for the departure of Alma and the people who went with him, who had formed a church of God through the strength, power, and faith based on Abinadi's words.
They mourned their departure, not knowing where they had gone. They would have gladly joined them, for they had also entered into a covenant with God to serve him and keep his commandments.
Since Ammon's arrival, King Limhi had also entered into a covenant with God, and so had many of his people, to serve him and keep his commandments.
King Limhi and many of his people wanted to be baptized, but there was no one in the land with authority from God. Ammon declined, considering himself an unworthy servant.
So they did not form a church at that time but waited upon the Spirit of the Lord. They wanted to become like Alma and his brothers who had fled into the wilderness.
They wanted to be baptized as a witness and testimony that they were willing to serve God with all their hearts. But they put it off for a time. An account of their baptism will be given later.
Now all the effort of Ammon, his people, King Limhi, and his people was focused on delivering themselves from the hands of the Lamanites and from bondage.

22.text: Ammon and King Limhi began to consult with the people about how to free themselves from bondage. They gathered all the people together to get their opinions on the matter.

They could find no way to free themselves except to take their women, children, flocks, herds, and tents and escape into the wilderness. The Lamanites were so numerous that it was impossible for Limhi's people to fight their way to freedom.
Gideon stood before the king and said: O king, you have listened to my words many times when we were fighting the Lamanites.

O king, if you have not found me to be a useless servant, and if you have listened to my words at all and they have been helpful, then please listen to my words now. I will be your servant and deliver this people from bondage.
The king allowed him to speak. Gideon said:

There is a back pass through the back wall on the back side of the city. The Lamanite guards get drunk at night. Let us send a proclamation among all our people to gather their flocks and herds and drive them into the wilderness by night.
I will go and pay the last tribute of wine to the Lamanites, and they will be drunk. We will pass through the secret pass to the left of their camp while they are drunk and asleep.
We will leave with our women, children, flocks, and herds and travel around the land of Shilom.
The king listened to Gideon's words.
King Limhi had his people gather their flocks. He sent the tribute of wine to the Lamanites and also sent extra wine as a gift. They drank freely of the wine King Limhi sent.
The people of King Limhi left by night into the wilderness with their flocks and herds. They went around the land of Shilom and headed toward the land of Zarahemla, led by Ammon and his brothers.
They took all their gold, silver, and precious things they could carry, along with their food supplies, and headed into the wilderness to continue their journey.
After many days in the wilderness, they arrived in the land of Zarahemla, joined Mosiah's people, and became his subjects.
Mosiah received them with joy. He also received their records and the records found by Limhi's people.
When the Lamanites found that Limhi's people had left the land by night, they sent an army into the wilderness to pursue them.
After pursuing them for two days, they could no longer follow their tracks. They became lost in the wilderness.

23.text: Alma, having been warned by the Lord that King Noah's armies would come, told his people. They gathered their flocks, took their grain, and fled into the wilderness before the armies arrived.
The Lord strengthened them so King Noah's people could not overtake them.
They fled eight days' journey into the wilderness.
They came to a very beautiful and pleasant land — a land of pure water.
They set up their tents, began working the ground, and started building. They were industrious and worked very hard.
The people wanted Alma to be their king because they loved him.

But he said: It is not right for us to have a king. For the Lord says: You shall not value one person above another, and no one should think himself above another. Therefore I say to you, it is not right for you to have a king.
However, if it were possible to always have just men as your kings, it would be good to have a king.
But remember the wickedness of King Noah and his priests. I myself was caught in a snare and did many abominable things in the sight of the Lord, which caused me deep repentance.
Nevertheless, after much trouble, the Lord heard my cries, answered my prayers, and made me an instrument in his hands to bring many of you to a knowledge of his truth.
I do not boast about this, for I am unworthy to boast of myself.
Now I say to you, you were oppressed by King Noah and were in bondage to him and his priests and were led into sin by them. So you were bound by the bands of wickedness.

Since you have been delivered by the power of God from these bonds — from the hands of King Noah and his people, and from the bonds of wickedness — I want you to stand firm in this freedom and trust no man to be a king over you.
Also, trust no one to be your teacher or minister unless he is a man of God, walking in his ways and keeping his commandments.
Alma taught his people that every man should love his neighbor as himself and that there should be no conflict among them.
Alma was their high priest and the founder of their church.
No one received authority to preach or teach unless it came through Alma from God. He consecrated all their priests and teachers, and only just men were consecrated.
They watched over their people and nourished them with things pertaining to righteousness.
They began to prosper greatly in the land and called it Helam.
They multiplied and prospered greatly in the land of Helam. They built a city, which they called the city of Helam.
Nevertheless, the Lord sees fit to discipline his people. He tests their patience and faith.
But whoever puts his trust in him will be lifted up at the last day. And so it was with this people.
For I will show you that they were brought into bondage, and no one could deliver them but the Lord their God — the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.
He did deliver them and showed his mighty power to them, and great was their rejoicing.
While they were in the land of Helam, in the city of Helam, working the surrounding land, a Lamanite army appeared at the borders of the land.
Alma's brothers fled from their fields and gathered in the city of Helam. They were very frightened by the Lamanites.
Alma went among them and urged them not to be frightened but to remember the Lord their God, who would deliver them.
So they calmed their fears and began to cry to the Lord to soften the hearts of the Lamanites, that they would spare them, their wives, and their children.
The Lord softened the Lamanites' hearts. Alma and his brothers surrendered themselves to the Lamanites, who took possession of the land of Helam.
The Lamanite armies that had followed Limhi's people had gotten lost in the wilderness for many days.
They found the priests of King Noah in a place they called Amulon. The priests had begun to possess the land and work the soil.
The leader of the priests was named Amulon.
Amulon pleaded with the Lamanites. He also sent out their wives, who were daughters of the Lamanites, to plead with the Lamanites not to destroy their husbands.
The Lamanites had compassion on Amulon and his brothers because of their wives and did not destroy them.
Amulon and his brothers joined the Lamanites. While traveling through the wilderness searching for the land of Nephi, they discovered the land of Helam, which Alma and his brothers possessed.
The Lamanites promised Alma and his brothers that if they showed them the way to the land of Nephi, they would grant them their lives and their freedom.
But after Alma showed them the way to the land of Nephi, the Lamanites did not keep their promise. They set guards around the land of Helam, over Alma and his brothers.
The rest of the Lamanites went to the land of Nephi. Some returned to the land of Helam, bringing with them the wives and children of the guards who had been left behind.
The Lamanite king appointed Amulon as a king and ruler over his people in the land of Helam, though he could do nothing contrary to the will of the Lamanite king.

24.text: Amulon gained favor with the Lamanite king, who appointed him and his brothers as teachers over his people — the people in the land of Shemlon, Shilom, and Amulon.
The Lamanites had taken possession of all these lands. The Lamanite king had appointed kings over all of them.
The Lamanite king's name was Laman, named after his father. Therefore he was called King Laman. He was king over a numerous people.
He appointed Amulon's brothers as teachers in every land his people possessed. So the Nephite language began to be taught among all the Lamanites.

They were a people who were friendly with one another. However, they did not know God. Amulon's brothers did not teach them anything about the Lord their God, the law of Moses, or the words of Abinadi.
They only taught them to keep records and write to one another.

So the Lamanites began to increase in riches, trade with one another, and grow great. They became a cunning and wise people in worldly wisdom — a very cunning people who delighted in all kinds of wickedness and plunder, except among their own brothers.
Amulon began to exercise authority over Alma and his brothers. He persecuted them and had his children persecute their children.

Amulon knew Alma — that he had been one of the king's priests, that he was the one who believed Abinadi's words and was driven out by the king. So Amulon was angry with him. Though subject to King Laman, Amulon exercised authority over them, gave them tasks, and put taskmasters over them.
Their afflictions became so great that they began to cry mightily to God.
Amulon commanded them to stop their prayers. He put guards over them to watch — whoever was found calling upon God would be put to death.
Alma and his people did not raise their voices to the Lord their God but poured out their hearts to him, and he knew the thoughts of their hearts.

The voice of the Lord came to them in their afflictions, saying: Lift up your heads and be of good comfort. I know of the covenant you have made to me. I will make a covenant with my people and deliver them from bondage.

I will also lighten the burdens placed on your shoulders so that you cannot even feel them on your backs, even while you are in bondage. I will do this so you may stand as witnesses for me afterward, and so you may know for certain that I, the Lord God, visit my people in their afflictions.

The burdens placed on Alma and his brothers were made light. The Lord strengthened them so they could bear their burdens with ease. They submitted cheerfully and patiently to all the will of the Lord.
Their faith and patience were so great that the Lord's voice came to them again, saying: Be of good comfort. Tomorrow I will deliver you from bondage.
He said to Alma: You will go before this people, and I will go with you and deliver this people from bondage.
During the night, Alma and his people gathered their flocks and grain together — all night long they gathered their flocks.
In the morning, the Lord caused a deep sleep to fall on the Lamanites. All their taskmasters were in a deep sleep.
Alma and his people left for the wilderness. After traveling all day, they set up their tents in a valley. They called the valley Alma because he led the way through the wilderness.

In the valley of Alma, they poured out their thanks to God because he had been merciful to them, eased their burdens, and delivered them from bondage. They had been in bondage, and no one could have delivered them except the Lord their God.
They gave thanks to God — all their men, women, and children who could speak lifted their voices in praise of their God.

The Lord said to Alma: Hurry — get yourself and this people out of this land, for the Lamanites have woken up and are pursuing you. Get out of this land, and I will stop the Lamanites in this valley so they come no further after this people.
They left the valley and continued their journey into the wilderness.
After twelve days in the wilderness, they arrived in the land of Zarahemla. King Mosiah received them with joy.

25.text: King Mosiah had all the people gathered together.
There were not as many descendants of Nephi as there were of the people of Zarahemla, who descended from Mulek and those who came with him into the wilderness.
There were not as many Nephites and people of Zarahemla combined as there were Lamanites. They were not even half as numerous.
All the people of Nephi and all the people of Zarahemla were assembled together in two groups.
Mosiah read the records of Zeniff to his people — the records from when they left Zarahemla until they returned.
He also read the account of Alma and his brothers and all their afflictions, from when they left Zarahemla until they returned.
When Mosiah finished reading, the people who had stayed in the land were struck with wonder and amazement.
They did not know what to think. When they saw those who had been freed from bondage, they were filled with exceedingly great joy.
But when they thought of their brothers who had been killed by the Lamanites, they were filled with sorrow and shed many tears.
When they thought about the immediate goodness of God and his power in delivering Alma and his brothers from the Lamanites and from bondage, they raised their voices and gave thanks to God.
When they thought about the Lamanites, who were their brothers, and their sinful and polluted state, they were filled with pain and anguish for the welfare of their souls.

The children of Amulon and his brothers — those who had married the daughters of the Lamanites — were displeased with their fathers' conduct and no longer wanted to be called by their fathers' names. They took on the name of Nephi so they could be called the children of Nephi and be numbered with the Nephites.
All the people of Zarahemla were numbered with the Nephites because the kingdom had been given only to descendants of Nephi.
When Mosiah finished speaking and reading to the people, he asked Alma to also speak to the people.
Alma spoke to them as they were assembled in large groups. He went from one group to another, preaching repentance and faith in the Lord.
He encouraged the people of Limhi and his brothers — all who had been delivered from bondage — to remember that it was the Lord who delivered them.
After Alma had taught the people many things and finished speaking, King Limhi wanted to be baptized, and all his people wanted to be baptized too.
Alma went into the water and baptized them just as he had baptized his brothers in the waters of Mormon. Everyone he baptized belonged to the church of God because of their belief in Alma's words.
King Mosiah allowed Alma to establish churches throughout the land of Zarahemla and gave him authority to ordain priests and teachers over every church.
This was necessary because there were so many people that they could not all be governed by one teacher, nor could they all hear the word of God in one assembly.
So they assembled in different groups called churches. Each church had its own priests and teachers, and each priest preached the word as it was delivered to him by Alma.
Even though there were many churches, they were all one church — the church of God. Nothing was preached in any of the churches except repentance and faith in God.
There were seven churches in the land of Zarahemla. Whoever wanted to take upon them the name of Christ, or of God, joined the churches of God.
They were called the people of God. The Lord poured out his Spirit upon them, and they were blessed and prospered in the land.

26.text: Many of the rising generation could not understand King Benjamin's words because they were little children when he spoke. They did not believe the traditions of their fathers.
They did not believe what had been said about the resurrection of the dead, nor did they believe in the coming of Christ.
Because of their unbelief, they could not understand the word of God, and their hearts were hardened.
They would not be baptized or join the church. They were a separate people in their faith and remained in their sinful state. They would not call upon the Lord their God.
During Mosiah's reign, they were not half as numerous as the people of God. But because of divisions among the believers, they grew more numerous.
They deceived many church members with flattering words and caused them to commit many sins. It became necessary for the church to deal with those who sinned.
They were brought before the priests, who were delivered to them by the teachers. The priests brought them before Alma, the high priest.
King Mosiah had given Alma authority over the church.
Alma did not know what to do about them. But there were many witnesses against them — the people stood and testified of their sins in abundance.
Nothing like this had happened before in the church, so Alma was troubled in his spirit. He brought them before the king.

He said to the king: We have brought many before you who are accused by their brothers. They have been caught in various sins and do not repent. We have brought them to you so you can judge them for their crimes.
But King Mosiah said to Alma: I will not judge them. I give them to you to judge.
Alma's spirit was troubled again. He went and asked the Lord what to do, fearing he might do wrong in God's sight.
After he poured out his whole soul to God, the voice of the Lord came to him, saying:
Blessed are you, Alma, and blessed are those who were baptized in the waters of Mormon. You are blessed because of your exceedingly great faith in the words of my servant Abinadi alone.
Blessed are they because of their exceedingly great faith in the words you alone spoke to them.
Blessed are you because you have established a church among this people. They will be established, and they will be my people.
Blessed are this people who are willing to bear my name. In my name they will be called, and they are mine.
Because you asked me about the transgressor, you are blessed.
You are my servant. I covenant with you that you will have eternal life. You will serve me, go forth in my name, and gather my sheep together.
Whoever will hear my voice will be my sheep. You will receive him into the church, and I will also receive him.
For this is my church. Whoever is baptized shall be baptized unto repentance. Whoever you receive shall believe in my name, and I will freely forgive him.
For it is I who take upon me the sins of the world. It is I who created them. It is I who grant to whoever believes to the end a place at my right hand.
For they are called by my name. If they know me, they will come forth and have a place eternally at my right hand.
When the second trumpet sounds, those who never knew me will come forth and stand before me.
Then they will know that I am the Lord their God, their Redeemer. But they would not be redeemed.
Then I will confess to them that I never knew them, and they will depart into everlasting fire prepared for the devil and his angels.
Therefore I say to you, whoever will not hear my voice, do not receive him into my church, for I will not receive him at the last day.

Therefore I say to you: Go. Whoever transgresses against me, judge him according to the sins he has committed. If he confesses his sins before you and me and repents in sincerity of heart, forgive him, and I will forgive him also.
As often as my people repent, I will forgive them their trespasses against me.
You must also forgive one another your trespasses. Truly I say to you, whoever does not forgive his neighbor when his neighbor says he repents brings condemnation upon himself.
Now I say to you: Go. Whoever will not repent of his sins shall not be numbered among my people. This shall be observed from this time forward.
When Alma heard these words, he wrote them down so he could judge the people of the church according to God's commandments.
Alma judged those who had been caught in sin according to the word of the Lord.
Whoever repented of their sins and confessed them were numbered among the people of the church.
Those who would not confess their sins and repent were not counted among the people of the church, and their names were removed.
Alma organized all the affairs of the church, and they began to have peace and prosper greatly in the church, walking carefully before God, receiving many, and baptizing many.

Alma and his fellow workers who led the church walked in all diligence, teaching the word of God in all things, suffering all kinds of afflictions, and being persecuted by all who did not belong to the church of God.

They admonished their brothers. They were also admonished themselves — everyone by the word of God, according to his sins. They were commanded by God to pray without ceasing and give thanks in all things.

27.text: The persecution from unbelievers became so great that the church began to complain to their leaders about it. They complained to Alma, and Alma brought the matter before King Mosiah. Mosiah consulted with his priests.
King Mosiah sent a proclamation throughout the land that no unbeliever should persecute anyone who belonged to the church of God.
A strict command was given throughout all the churches that there should be no persecution among them and that there should be equality among all people.
No pride or arrogance should disturb their peace. Every person should value his neighbor as himself, working with their own hands to support themselves.
All priests and teachers should work with their own hands for their support, except in cases of sickness or great need. By doing this, they abounded in the grace of God.
Much peace returned to the land. The people became very numerous, spreading across the earth in all directions, building large cities and villages everywhere.
The Lord visited and prospered them, and they became a large and wealthy people.

The sons of Mosiah were numbered among the unbelievers. Also one of Alma's sons, named Alma after his father, was among them. He became a very wicked and idolatrous man. He was a man of many words and spoke much flattery to the people, leading many to follow his wicked ways.
He became a great obstacle to the prosperity of the church, stealing away the hearts of the people, causing much conflict, and giving the enemy of God a chance to exercise power over them.

While he was going about trying to destroy the church of God — for he went about secretly with the sons of Mosiah to destroy the church and lead the people of the Lord astray, contrary to the commandments of God and the king —
as they were going about rebelling against God, the angel of the Lord appeared to them. He descended in a cloud and spoke with a voice like thunder, which caused the earth to shake beneath them.
Their astonishment was so great that they fell to the earth and did not understand the words he spoke.

Nevertheless he cried again: Alma, arise and stand forth! Why do you persecute the church of God? For the Lord has said: This is my church, and I will establish it. Nothing will overthrow it except the transgression of my people.

And again the angel said: The Lord has heard the prayers of his people, and also the prayers of his servant Alma, who is your father. He has prayed with much faith about you, that you might come to know the truth. For this reason I have come — to convince you of the power and authority of God, so the prayers of his servants may be answered according to their faith.
Can you dispute the power of God? Does not my voice shake the earth? Can you not see me standing before you? I am sent from God.

Now I say to you: Go, and remember the captivity of your fathers in the land of Helam and the land of Nephi. Remember what great things he did for them — they were in bondage, and he delivered them. Now I say to you, Alma, go your way. Stop trying to destroy the church, so that their prayers may be answered — even if you yourself choose to be cast off.
These were the last words the angel spoke to Alma, and he departed.

Alma and those with him fell to the earth again, for great was their astonishment. They had seen an angel of the Lord with their own eyes. His voice was like thunder and shook the earth. They knew nothing but the power of God could shake the earth and make it tremble as if it would split apart.
Alma's astonishment was so great that he became mute and could not open his mouth. He became so weak he could not move his hands. Those with him carried him, helpless, and laid him before his father.
They told his father everything that had happened. His father rejoiced, knowing it was the power of God.
He gathered a multitude to witness what the Lord had done for his son and for those who were with him.

He had the priests assemble. They began to fast and pray to the Lord that Alma's mouth might be opened so he could speak, and that his limbs might regain their strength — that the people might see and know the goodness and glory of God.
After they had fasted and prayed for two days and two nights, Alma's limbs regained their strength. He stood up and began to speak to them, telling them to be of good comfort.
He said: I have repented of my sins and have been redeemed by the Lord. I am born of the Spirit.

The Lord said to me: Do not marvel that all mankind — men and women, all nations, families, languages, and peoples — must be born again. Yes, born of God, changed from their sinful and fallen state to a state of righteousness, being redeemed of God, becoming his sons and daughters.
Thus they become new creatures. Unless they do this, they can in no way inherit the kingdom of God.
I say to you, unless this happens, they must be cast off. I know this because I was about to be cast off.
Nevertheless, after wading through much tribulation, repenting nearly to the point of death, the Lord in his mercy saw fit to snatch me out of an everlasting burning. I am born of God.

My soul has been redeemed from the bitterness and bonds of sin. I was in the darkest abyss, but now I see the marvelous light of God. My soul was racked with eternal torment, but I am snatched out, and my soul is pained no more.
I rejected my Redeemer and denied what our fathers had spoken. But now they may foresee that he will come and that he remembers every creature of his creation. He will make himself manifest to all.

Every knee will bow, and every tongue will confess before him. At the last day, when all people stand to be judged, they will confess that he is God. Then those who lived without God in the world will confess that the judgment of everlasting punishment is just upon them. They will quake, tremble, and shrink beneath the glance of his all-searching eye.

From that time forward, Alma began to teach the people. Those who were with Alma when the angel appeared traveled throughout the land, telling everyone what they had heard and seen, preaching the word of God in much tribulation, being greatly persecuted by unbelievers and struck by many of them.
But despite all this, they brought much comfort to the church, strengthening their faith and urging them with patience and long-suffering to keep the commandments of God.
Four of them were sons of Mosiah: Ammon, Aaron, Omner, and Himni.

They traveled throughout the land of Zarahemla and among all the people under King Mosiah, zealously working to repair all the damage they had done to the church. They confessed all their sins and proclaimed everything they had seen, explaining the prophecies and scriptures to all who wanted to hear.
They were instruments in the hands of God, bringing many to a knowledge of the truth — to the knowledge of their Redeemer.
How blessed they were! For they proclaimed peace and good tidings, and declared to the people that the Lord reigns.

28.text: After the sons of Mosiah had done all these things, they took a small group and returned to their father the king. They asked him to allow them and those they had chosen to go to the land of Nephi to preach what they had heard and share the word of God with their brothers the Lamanites.

Perhaps they could bring the Lamanites to know the Lord their God and convince them of their fathers' sins, and perhaps cure them of their hatred toward the Nephites so they could also rejoice in the Lord and become friendly, with no more conflicts in the land the Lord had given them.
They wanted salvation declared to every creature. They could not bear the thought of any human soul perishing. The very idea that any soul should endure endless torment made them quake and tremble.

The Spirit of the Lord worked upon them in this way, for they had been the very worst of sinners. The Lord saw fit in his infinite mercy to spare them. Nevertheless, they suffered much anguish of soul because of their sins, suffering greatly and fearing they would be cast off forever.
They pleaded with their father for many days to let them go to the land of Nephi.
King Mosiah went and asked the Lord if he should let his sons go among the Lamanites to preach the word.
The Lord said to Mosiah: Let them go, for many will believe their words and have eternal life. I will deliver your sons from the hands of the Lamanites.
Mosiah allowed them to go and do as they requested.
They set out on their journey into the wilderness to preach the word among the Lamanites. An account of their activities will be given later.
King Mosiah had no one to give the kingdom to, since none of his sons would accept it.

So he took the records engraved on the brass plates, the plates of Nephi, and everything he had kept and preserved according to God's commandments. He had also translated and had written down the records from the gold plates that Limhi's people had found and given to him.
He did this because of his people's intense desire to know about the people who had been destroyed.
He translated them using two stones fastened into the two rims of a bow.
These things were prepared from the beginning and handed down from generation to generation for the purpose of interpreting languages.
They were kept and preserved by the hand of the Lord, so he could reveal to every people who possessed the land the sins and abominations of his people.
Whoever possesses these things is called a seer, after the manner of old times.

After Mosiah finished translating these records, they told the story of the people who were destroyed — going back from the time of their destruction to the building of the great tower, when the Lord confused the languages and the people were scattered across the earth, and even further back to the creation of Adam.
This account caused Mosiah's people to mourn greatly. They were filled with sorrow. But it also gave them much knowledge, which made them rejoice.
This account will be written later, for it is important that all people know what is written in it.

After King Mosiah had done these things, he took the brass plates, all the things he had kept, and gave them to Alma, son of Alma. He gave him all the records and the interpreters and commanded him to keep and preserve them, and to keep a record of the people, passing them down from one generation to another, just as they had been handed down since Lehi left Jerusalem.

29.text: After Mosiah had done this, he sent a message throughout the land among all the people, wanting to know who they wanted as their king.
The people responded, saying: We want Aaron your son to be our king and ruler.
But Aaron had gone to the land of Nephi, so the king could not give him the kingdom. Aaron would not have accepted it anyway, nor would any of the other sons of Mosiah.
So King Mosiah sent another message to the people — a written message. These are the words he wrote:
My people — my brothers, for that is how I regard you — I want you to consider the matter before you, for you want to have a king.
I declare to you that the one who rightfully should be king has declined and will not take the kingdom.

If someone else were appointed in his place, I fear conflicts would arise among you. Who knows whether my son, to whom the kingdom rightfully belongs, might become angry and draw some people after him, which would cause wars and conflicts, much bloodshed, perverting of the Lord's ways, and destruction of many souls.
Let us be wise and consider these things. We have no right to destroy my son, nor would we have the right to destroy another if he were appointed in his place.
If my son should return to pride and vanity, he would take back what he said and claim his right to the kingdom, causing him and this people to commit much sin.
Let us be wise and look ahead, and do what will make for the peace of this people.

Therefore I will be your king for the rest of my days. But let us appoint judges to judge this people according to our law. We will reorganize the affairs of this people by appointing wise men as judges, who will judge according to God's commandments.
It is better for a person to be judged by God than by man, because God's judgments are always just, but man's judgments are not always just.

If it were possible to always have just men as your kings who would establish God's laws and judge according to his commandments — if you could have men for your kings who would do as my father Benjamin did for this people — then it would be good for you to always have kings to rule over you.

Even I myself have labored with all my power and abilities to teach you God's commandments and establish peace throughout the land, so there would be no wars, conflicts, stealing, plundering, murdering, or any kind of wickedness.
Whoever has committed a crime, I have punished according to the law given to us by our fathers.
Now I say to you: because not all people are just, it is not good for you to have a king or kings.
For consider how much wickedness one wicked king causes, and what great destruction!

Remember King Noah — his wickedness and abominations, and also the wickedness and abominations of his people. See what great destruction came upon them! Because of their sins, they were brought into bondage.
If it were not for the intervention of their all-wise Creator — because of their sincere repentance — they would have inevitably remained in bondage until now.

But he delivered them because they humbled themselves before him. Because they cried mightily to him, he delivered them from bondage. This is how the Lord works with his power among the children of men, extending the arm of mercy to those who trust in him.
You cannot dethrone a wicked king without much conflict and bloodshed.
For he has his friends in wickedness. He keeps his guards around him. He tears up the laws of righteous rulers who came before him. He tramples God's commandments under his feet.

He makes laws and sends them among the people — laws according to his own wickedness. Whoever does not obey his laws, he has destroyed. Whoever rebels against him, he sends his armies to make war against them. If he can, he destroys them. This is how an unrighteous king perverts all the ways of righteousness.
I say to you, it is not right for such abominations to come upon you.
Therefore choose judges by the voice of this people, so you may be judged according to the laws given to you by our fathers — which are correct and were given to them by the hand of the Lord.

It is not common for the voice of the people to choose what is wrong. But it is common for the minority to want what is not right. Therefore make it your law to do business by the voice of the people.

If the time comes when the voice of the people chooses wickedness, then the judgments of God will come upon you. Then he will visit you with great destruction, just as he has done in this land before.
If you have judges and they do not judge according to the law, you can have them judged by a higher judge.
If your higher judges do not judge righteously, have a small number of your lower judges gather and judge the higher judges, according to the voice of the people.
I command you to do these things in the fear of the Lord. I command you to have no king, so that if this people commits sins and wickedness, they will be responsible for their own actions.
For the sins of many people have been caused by the wickedness of their kings. Therefore their sins are upon the heads of their kings.

I want this inequality to exist no more in this land, especially among my people. I want this land to be a land of liberty where every person enjoys his rights and privileges equally, as long as the Lord sees fit for us to live and inherit the land — as long as any of our descendants remain on the face of the land.

King Mosiah wrote many more things to them, explaining all the trials and troubles of a righteous king, all the burdens on his soul for his people, and all the people's complaints to their king. He explained it all.
He told them this should not be — that the burden should be shared by all the people, with every man bearing his part.
He also explained all the disadvantages of having an unrighteous king rule over them.

All the wickedness and abominations, wars, conflicts, bloodshed, stealing, plundering, immorality, and all kinds of sins that cannot be counted — he told them these things should not exist, that they were completely opposed to God's commandments.
After King Mosiah sent these things among the people, they were convinced of the truth of his words.
They gave up their desires for a king and became very eager for every person to have an equal chance throughout the land. Every person was willing to answer for his own sins.
They assembled in groups throughout the land to cast their votes for who should be their judges. They were exceedingly joyful about the freedom that had been granted to them.

Their love for Mosiah grew strong. They valued him more than any other man. They did not see him as a tyrant seeking gain or wealth that corrupts the soul. He had not taken riches from them or delighted in bloodshed. He had established peace in the land and freed his people from all kinds of bondage. Therefore they valued him exceedingly, beyond measure.
They appointed judges to rule over them, or to judge them according to the law. They did this throughout the land.
Alma was appointed the first chief judge. He was also the high priest, as his father had given him that office and charged him with all the affairs of the church.
Alma walked in the ways of the Lord, kept his commandments, and made righteous judgments. There was continual peace throughout the land.
Thus began the reign of the judges throughout all the land of Zarahemla, among all the people called the Nephites. Alma was the first and chief judge.
His father died at the age of eighty-two, having lived to fulfill the commandments of God.
Mosiah also died in the thirty-third year of his reign, at the age of sixty-three — making a total of five hundred and nine years from the time Lehi left Jerusalem.
Thus ended the reign of the kings over the people of Nephi, and thus ended the days of Alma, the founder of their church.
